serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Murgh Cheese Kebab is approximately 200 g, comprising several pieces of tandoor-grilled boneless chicken served on a bed of spiced bell pepper.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken tikka / tandoori chicken (grilled, marinated) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171477, Chicken breast, grilled) for micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for murgh cheese kebab exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken / chicken tikka data (per 100 g edible portion) and adjusted upward for fat and energy to account for the cheese and cashew paste marinade. Protein values are anchored to ICMR-NIN grilled boneless chicken. Micronutrients supplemented from USDA FDC grilled chicken breast where ICMR-NIN does not report the field.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — grilled chicken / protein-dominant mixed dish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for murgh cheese kebab. GI estimated at 30 based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled chicken dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dish is protein-dominant (~22.5 g/100 g) with low carbohydrate (~4.2 g/100 g), consistent with a low GI. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 4.2 × 2) / 100 = 2.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~840 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as chutneys or pickles, as a 200 g serving contributes approximately 840 mg sodium, representing about 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Murgh Cheese Kebab has a low glycemic index of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of only 2.5 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese and cashew paste (~3.6 g/100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cheese and cashew paste marinade contributes approximately 3.6 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to one moderate serving and pair with non-starchy vegetables to keep total saturated fat within the recommended 20 g daily ceiling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murgh Cheese Kebab is a North Indian tandoor-grilled chicken dish in which boneless chicken pieces are marinated in a rich blend of cheese, cashew paste, cardamom, and aromatic spices before being cooked in a traditional clay tandoor oven at high heat. The result is a succulent, lightly charred kebab with a creamy, mildly nutty flavour profile and a tender, juicy texture. The dish is served on a bed of spiced bell pepper, adding colour, crunch, and a gentle heat. Nutritionally, it is protein-dense (approximately 22.5 g protein per 100 g) and relatively low in carbohydrates (approximately 4.2 g per 100 g), giving it a low glycemic index of around 30 and a glycemic load of only 2.5 per standard 200 g restaurant serving. The fat content is moderate (~9.8 g/100 g), with saturated fat elevated by the cheese component (~3.6 g/100 g). Sodium is notable at approximately 420 mg/100 g due to the marinade and cheese. Clinically, this dish is well-suited for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes owing to its low GI and high protein content, but those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should be mindful of sodium and saturated fat intake. It is naturally gluten-free and commonly prepared as a halal dish. In Singapore, it is served at North Indian restaurants and is popular as a starter or sharing platter.
common_user_queries: Is murgh cheese kebab good for diabetics?
Is murgh cheese kebab gluten free?
What is in murgh cheese kebab marinade?
Murgh cheese kebab vs chicken tikka
North Indian kebab with cheese and cashew

confidence_reason: No exact entry for murgh cheese kebab exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken / chicken tikka data with upward adjustment for fat and energy to account for the cheese and cashew paste marinade. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. The input food_name contained a typographical error ('Chesse' instead of 'Cheese'); corrected to 'Murgh Cheese Kebab' as the canonical English name. 2. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken / chicken tikka (per 100 g edible portion) with upward adjustment for fat (+2 g/100 g) and energy (+20 kcal/100 g) to account for the cheese and cashew paste marinade; these are not exact measurements. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category and is not reliably available from USDA for this adapted preparation. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC grilled chicken breast (FDC ID 171477) as a supplementary reference where ICMR-NIN does not report these fields for this preparation. 5. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for murgh cheese kebab was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the typical preparation at North Indian halal-certified restaurants in Singapore; not independently verified for this specific outlet. 7. The dish is served at Bombay Curry And Kebabs North Indian Cuisine, South Bridge Road, Singapore (field survey source). 8. All -1 values represent nutrients not reported in the primary or supplementary approved sources for this adapted preparation.
